papers.title_en: Optimal Communication Rate of Secure Aggregation over Ring Networks with Pairwise Keys
papers.authors: Xiang Zhang, Han Yu, Zhou Li, Yizhou Zhao, Giuseppe Caire
papers.evidence: 消除安全分布式聚合中的可信密钥服务器
papers.tldr: 本文研究了环形网络拓扑下基于两两共享密钥的信息论安全聚合（TSA）问题，旨在消除传统方案对中心化密钥服务器的依赖。研究刻画了K个用户在环形拓扑下的最小每用户通信率，发现当K为3或4时通信率为1，而当K大于等于5时通信率提升至2。通过提出一种线性两两掩码方案并结合熵逆界证明，本文揭示了网络规模扩大导致的密钥对齐约束冲突，并发现仅需特定距离的用户对密钥即可实现最优通信。
papers.abstract_en: Information-theoretic topological secure aggregation (TSA)\cite{zhang2026information_regular} enables distributed users to compute neighborhood sums over arbitrary networks without revealing individual inputs, while remaining communication-efficient. It has broad applications, including secure model aggregation in decentralized federated learning (FL). Existing TSA formulations rely on arbitrarily correlated keys generated by a trusted key server, which introduces a single point of failure. In this paper, we instead study TSA with \tit{pairwise} secret keys, where each user pair $(i,j)$ shares an independent key $S_{i,j}$. Such keys can be established through inter-user communication, eliminating the need for a key server and improving robustness. Focusing on a ring topology with $K$ users, we characterize the minimum per-user communication rate: \tit{to securely compute one bit of the desired input sum, each user must send at least $1$ bit to its neighbors when $K=3,4$, and at least $2$ bits for all $K\ge 5$}. The higher rate in larger networks arises because each user must simultaneously satisfy two independent key-alignment constraints from its two neighborhoods, which cannot be resolved within a single broadcast symbol under pairwise key independence. We propose a linear pairwise-masking scheme that achieves these rates and prove its optimality via tight entropic converse bounds that exploit the dependency structure of the keys. Notably, for all $K\ge 4$, only a subset of the $\binom{K}{2}$ pairwise keys -- specifically, those between users at ring distance $2$ -- is sufficient to achieve optimality, revealing a nontrivial role of topological sparsity in secure aggregation.
